nativeRange.habitat: The Lombardoi Mbuna inhabits the rocky shoreline and intermediate zones around Mbenji Island in Lake Malawi, living among sediment-free boulder fields at moderate depths — the same hard, structured habitat favored by mbuna cichlids across the lake.

decorPreferences: large piles of rocks forming caves and crevices
open sandy substrate between rock structures
minimal or no plants (uprooted by digging)
dense rockwork to break line of sight and distribute aggression

diet.notes: In the wild, the Lombardoi Mbuna is primarily herbivorous, grazing on aufwuchs (the layer of algae and microorganisms that encrust rocky surfaces). In the aquarium, spirulina-based flakes or pellets should form the staple diet, supplemented with vegetable matter such as blanched zucchini or spinach, high-quality cichlid pellets, and occasional frozen foods like mysis or brine shrimp. High-protein meaty foods should be offered sparingly, as a diet heavy in animal protein is associated with Malawi bloat — a serious internal condition linked to dietary stress and declining water quality in mbuna. Small meals offered two to three times daily are generally preferable to a single large feeding.

goodWith: other robust Lake Malawi mbuna cichlids of similar size (e.g., Pseudotropheus, Labeotropheus, Melanochromis)
kept in species colonies or with equally aggressive mbuna
large, fast-moving non-cichlid dither fish if tank is very large
avoidWith: peaceful or semi-aggressive fish of any kind
smaller or less aggressive cichlid species
invertebrates (shrimp, snails will be harassed or eaten)
Haplochromine cichlids (Haps/Peacocks) unless tank is very large — mbuna will relentlessly harass
conspecific males kept together in small tanks (extreme male–male aggression)

imageCandidates.notes: Toby Hudson; 3888x2592. Tack-sharp adult male in bright yellow-orange nuptial colour with faint barring against a dark background, whole fish in frame — the iconic male look and clearest diagnostic shot.
Vlad Butsky (filed under synonym Pseudotropheus lombardoi); 2192x1624. Sharp, well-lit yellow male showing the prominent dark vertical bars an active/dominant male displays over the yellow ground.

Steven Viemeister; 1024x820. Sharp specimen in the powder-blue phase with crisp black vertical barring, illustrating the reversed sexual dichromatism (female/juvenile colouration) noted in the summary. Lower resolution than the male shots.

summary: The Lombardoi Mbuna is a Lake Malawi endemic from Mbenji Island, widely considered one of the most aggressive mbuna in the hobby. It is notable for reversed sexual coloration: females are powder blue with dark barring while males develop bright yellow-orange. A maternal mouthbrooder requiring advanced care.
careNotes:
The Lombardoi Mbuna is widely regarded as one of the most belligerent mbuna species in the hobby and demands advanced-level husbandry focused almost entirely on managing extreme aggression. A minimum 300 L tank (150 cm footprint) is recommended, with larger setups strongly preferred. The aquarium must be heavily rockscaped with numerous caves, overhangs, and sight-line breaks — aggression tends to concentrate on any individual that cannot escape or hide, and a fish trapped in the open is at serious risk. Keeping a larger colony of 8–12 or more individuals is a well-established mbuna strategy for diluting aggression across multiple targets, reducing the likelihood of a single subordinate fish being singled out and hounded.

This species exhibits reversed sexual coloration compared to most other mbuna: females are powder blue with dark vertical barring — the pattern most mbuna males display — while males develop bright yellow to orange-yellow coloration with faint barring. Juveniles of both sexes start out blue and are visually identical; males transition to yellow as they mature. This means juveniles are sometimes mislabeled as female at the point of sale, and buyers should be aware that blue fish may color up yellow over time.

The genus name applied to this species is contested. Stauffer (2004) placed it in Maylandia; Konings and Stauffer (2018) maintained Metriaclima as the valid genus, arguing that Maylandia is a junior synonym. Both names remain in active use across the scientific and hobbyist literature — FishBase and SeriouslyFish currently list the species as Metriaclima lombardoi, while a portion of the hobby continues to use Maylandia. Either name refers to the same fish, and the debate is ongoing.

Water chemistry should match Lake Malawi conditions: pH 7.8–8.5, gH 10–20, kH 8–16, and temperature 24–28°C. Weekly water changes of around 25–30% help export nitrates that accumulate at the high stocking densities typically used to manage aggression. Malawi bloat — an internal condition associated with dietary stress and declining water quality — is a genuine risk with this species; a primarily herbivorous diet and careful avoidance of overfeeding meaty proteins are generally advised. Lake Malawi salt additives or crushed coral in the substrate can help maintain carbonate hardness and buffer pH over time.
breedingNotes: The Lombardoi Mbuna is a maternal mouthbrooder. Spawning takes place on flat rocks or open substrate following a typical cichlid egg-spot courtship display, during which the female pecks at the egg-spot markings on the male's anal fin and takes up sperm. She then collects and orally incubates 15–30 eggs for approximately 21–28 days at 26°C, during which she does not feed. Fry are released as fully formed, free-swimming juveniles measuring 8–12 mm and begin feeding immediately on crushed flake or finely ground cichlid pellet. Moving the holding female to a separate, low-aggression tank is generally advisable during incubation — stress in a communal mbuna setup can cause the female to spit the brood prematurely. Spawning itself tends to happen readily in stable, established mbuna colonies, but successfully raising fry requires this additional effort, placing the overall breeding challenge at a moderate level. All juveniles are blue at birth regardless of sex; males begin their transition to yellow coloration at around three to four months of age.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Metriaclima lombardoi
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided native range (Mbenji Island, Lake Malawi), adult size (~14 cm), water parameters (pH 7.5–8.8, temperature 23–26°C), diet (aufwuchs grazer/omnivore), aggression level, reversed sexual dichromatism, mouthbrooder details, and minimum tank size guidance. Species listed under Metriaclima lombardoi per FishBase/SeriouslyFish current nomenclature; Maylandia lombardoi per Stauffer 2004 — both names used in hobby literature.

additional.name: FishBase — Metriaclima lombardoi
additional.notes: Cross-referenced taxonomy (Cichlidae, Perciformes), adult size (males to 14 cm TL), native range (Mbenji Island endemic), depth range, and environmental parameters. FishBase currently lists the species as Metriaclima lombardoi, noting Maylandia lombardoi as a synonym (Stauffer 2004); Konings & Stauffer (2018) retain Metriaclima. Used Maylandia in id/scientificName per task specification.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — African Cichlid Care
additional.notes: General Lake Malawi mbuna husbandry guidance; confirmed herbivore-leaning diet requirements, Malawi bloat risk, tank size recommendations (55+ gallons for mbuna), rockscape strategy, and water parameter targets for the Lake Malawi biotope.

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — Lake Malawi cichlids
additional.notes: Confirmed reversed sexual dichromatism (females blue, males yellow/orange) and the species' reputation as one of the most aggressive mbuna; supported the overstocking dilution strategy for aggression management and rocky habitat requirements.

additional.name: Konings, A. & Stauffer, J.R. (2018) — Nomenclature review of Metriaclima/Maylandia
additional.notes: Documents the ongoing genus debate: Stauffer (2004) erected Maylandia; Konings & Stauffer (2018) argued Metriaclima has priority, making Maylandia a junior synonym. Both names remain in wide use. This entry uses Maylandia lombardoi per task specification but careNotes acknowledge the dispute.

fish.breedingNotes: A maternal mouthbrooder that incubates 15–30 eggs orally for approximately 21–28 days. Spawning tends to occur readily in stable mbuna colonies, but successfully raising fry depends on isolating the holding female to prevent stress-induced brood loss. All juveniles start out blue and males begin shifting to yellow at around three to four months of age.
